message.group-title: Endocrinology (including Diabetes Mellitus and Metabolic Disease)
message.abstract:
<jats:title>Summary</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>COVID-19 patients with chronic diseases such as hypertension, diabetes and coronary heart diseases is more likely to worsen, but with mixed results for COVID-19 severity. This meta-analysis is to analyze the correlation between hypertension, diabetes, coronary heart disease and COVID-19 disease severity.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Available data from PubMed, Web of Science, China National Knowledge Infrastructure Database, WanFang Database and VIP Database, were analyzed using a fixed effects model meta-analysis to derive overall odds ratios (
                    <jats:italic>OR</jats:italic>
                    ) with 95%
                    <jats:italic>CIs</jats:italic>
                    . Funnel plots and Begg’s were used to assess publication bias.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Findings</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Of 182 articles found following our initial search, we assessed 34 full-text articles, of which 9 articles with 1936 COVID-19 patients met all selection criteria for our meta-analysis. No significant heterogeneity between studies. There were significant correlations between COVID-19 severity and hypertension [
                    <jats:italic>OR</jats:italic>
                    =2.3 [95%
                    <jats:italic>CI</jats:italic>
                    (1.76, 3.00),
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    <0.01], diabetes [
                    <jats:italic>OR</jats:italic>
                    =2.67, 95%
                    <jats:italic>CI</jats:italic>
                    (1.91, 3.74),
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    <0.01], coronary heart disease [
                    <jats:italic>OR</jats:italic>
                    =2.85 [95%
                    <jats:italic>CI</jats:italic>
                    (1.68, 4.84),
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    <0.01]. Most of the studies in the funnel plot are on the upper part and few on the base part, and are roughly symmetrical left and right. Begg’s test: hypertension (
                    <jats:italic>Z</jats:italic>
                    =-0.1,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    =1.0), diabetes (
                    <jats:italic>Z</jats:italic>
                    =0.73,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    =0.466), coronary heart disease (
                    <jats:italic>Z</jats:italic>
                    =0.38,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    =0.707), all found no publication bias.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Interpretation</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Hypertension, diabetes, and coronary heart disease can affect the severity of COVID-19. It may be related to the imbalance of angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE2) and the cytokine storm induced by Glucolipid metabolic disorders (GLMD).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Funding</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81830113, 81530102); Major basic and applied basic research projects of Guangdong Province of China (No. 2019B030302005); National key R & D plan “Research on modernization of traditional Chinese medicine” (No. 2018YFC1704200) and Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (No. 2018A030313391)</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Effects of hypertension, diabetes and coronary heart disease on COVID-19 diseases severity: a systematic review and meta-analysis
